figures.name: Itzamna (Dew of Heaven / House of the Iguana)
figures.gloss: The elderly, supreme creator deity who rules the heavens and day. He is the first priest, the inventor of writing, the calendar, and agriculture, guiding humanity through esoteric cosmic mathematics rather than raw physical force.
figures.facet: Unlike thunder-hurling sky-fathers, he asserts that the universe is governed by profound calculation and ritual exactitude. He represents the scholarly, priestly ideal of Maya kingship, controlling creation through literacy and timekeeping.
figures.provenance: Diego de Landa, 'Relación de las cosas de Yucatán' (1566); Madrid Codex; Dresden Codex.

figures.name: Hunahpu & Xbalanque (The Hero Twins)
figures.gloss: The cunning, ball-playing brothers who descend into the underworld to avenge their murdered father. Surviving Xibalba's trap-filled houses, they defeat the Lords of Death, ascending to become the sun and moon.
figures.facet: They conquer death not by overpowering it, but by outperforming it. They voluntarily submit to the underworld's sacrificial oven, using their subsequent resurrection as an elaborate magic trick to coax the Death Lords into begging to be sacrificed themselves.
figures.provenance: Francisco Ximénez, translation of the 'Popol Vuh' (~1701); Classic Maya ceramic vessel iconography.

figures.name: Lords of Xibalba (One Death & Seven Death)
figures.gloss: The rulers of the subterranean Place of Fright, commanding lesser demons of disease, pus, and jaundice. They challenge noisy surface-dwellers to the Mesoamerican ballgame to defeat, sacrifice, and humiliate them.
figures.facet: They are entirely stripped of the solemn majesty usually afforded to death gods. They are vain, petty aristocratic bullies who cheat at sports—framing mortality not as cosmic justice, but as a predatory hazard that must be outsmarted.

figures.name: K'inich Ajaw (Sun-Faced Lord)
figures.gloss: The fierce, cross-eyed deity of the daytime sun. He requires a constant diet of royal blood and captive sacrifice to sustain his burning heat as he traverses the sky.
figures.facet: The sun is deeply predatory and inherently dual. At sunset, he transforms into the Jaguar God of the Underworld, fighting his way through the subterranean dark each night. His light is literally fueled by continuous warfare.
figures.provenance: Dresden Codex; Temple of the Sun inscriptions at Palenque; monumental stucco masks.

figures.name: Chaac (God of Rain and Lightning)
figures.gloss: The reptilian, goggle-eyed lord of storms who strikes the clouds with his lightning axe to release rain. He is simultaneously one entity and four distinct directional gods (the Chaacob) colored by the world's corners.
figures.facet: He physically embodies the fractal, chaotic nature of weather in the Yucatán. He must be constantly placated to prevent him from holding back life-giving rain or unleashing crop-destroying tempests, keeping humanity on a razor's edge of survival.
figures.provenance: Dresden Codex; Postclassic architectural masks at Uxmal and Chichen Itza.

figures.name: Kukulkan / Gucumatz (Sovereign Plumed Serpent)
figures.gloss: The creator deity who moves between the earth and the sky, commanding wind and water. He helped form humanity from maize and serves as the ultimate conduit between the human and divine realms.
figures.facet: In the Maya sphere, the feathered serpent is intimately tied to the 'vision serpent'—the actual physical manifestation of the ancestor or god that emerges from the smoke of burning, blood-soaked bark paper during royal bloodletting.
figures.provenance: Diego de Landa, 'Relación de las cosas de Yucatán'; 'Chilam Balam of Chumayel'; 'Popol Vuh'.

figures.name: Ix Chel (Lady Rainbow)
figures.gloss: The multifaceted goddess of the moon, weaving, midwifery, medicine, and catastrophic floods. She is often depicted as an elderly woman emptying a jug of water onto the earth.
figures.facet: She perfectly embodies the destructive paradox of water. She is the tender midwife of childbirth and the weaver of life, but simultaneously the apocalyptic crone who pours out the rotting, cataclysmic floods that end world ages.
figures.provenance: Dresden Codex; accounts of shrines at Cozumel and Isla Mujeres by early Spanish explorers.

figures.name: Wacah Chan (The World Tree)
figures.gloss: The colossal ceiba tree forming the central axis of the cosmos, its roots plunging deep into Xibalba and its branches holding up the thirteen levels of the heavens.
figures.facet: It is not a static geographic feature, but an active, temporary ritual conduit. Maya kings physically *become* the tree during bloodletting ceremonies, making the human sovereign the literal structural pillar holding reality together.
figures.provenance: Sarcophagus lid of K'inich Janaab Pakal (683 CE); San Bartolo murals.

figures.name: K'uh (Divine Essence)
figures.gloss: The animating, sacred life force that circulates through the cosmos, heavily concentrated in human blood, tree sap, incense smoke, and the breath of deities.
figures.facet: The Maya concept of the sacred is intensely corporeal. Divinity is a measurable, communicable liquid that must be physically extracted from humans and fed to the gods, who will literally starve and collapse without this caloric spiritual energy.
figures.provenance: David Stuart, 'The Blood of Kings' (1986); Stephen Houston, 'The Life Within' (2014).

edges.note: Intra-tradition: Often paired as the primordial creator couple of the uppermost heavens.
Intra-tradition: The ultimate cosmic rivalry, pitching human wit and athletic skill against the arrogant inevitability of death.
Intra-tradition: The twin engines of agricultural survival, balancing scorching solar heat with torrential rains.
Intra-tradition: The World Tree acts as the primary vascular structure through which the sacred blood-essence (k'uh) flows between realms.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The direct Maya expression of the pan-Mesoamerican feathered serpent creator.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The direct Maya equivalent of the Central Mexican storm and rain deity.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The specific Maya iteration of the pan-Mesoamerican lords of the underworld.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The specific Popol Vuh manifestation of the pan-Mesoamerican mythic twins.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Maya instantiation of the Mesoamerican central cosmic ceiba.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Shared Mesoamerican conceptualizations of a flowing, animating sacred energy.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Elderly, scholarly gods who invent writing and the calendar to establish cosmic order.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Creator deities who shape the cosmos through thought, speech, and esoteric knowledge rather than physical combat.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Storm gods who violently strike the heavens with an axe or hammer to produce thunder and lightning.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Martial weather deities essential for releasing the waters needed for agricultural survival.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Solar sovereigns who rule the sky by day and must violently battle their way through the underworld each night.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Cunning tricksters who rely on illusion, shape-shifting, and sleight-of-hand to undo their more powerful adversaries.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Subterranean sovereigns commanding the dismal realm where the dead dissolve in darkness.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Rulers of a dangerous, trap-laden underworld who strip descending heroes or deities of their power and vitality.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Fearsome mother goddesses who embody both vital terrestrial creation and terrifying, unyielding destruction.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The colossal cosmic tree that spans the heavens, the mortal plane, and the underworld, binding the universe together.
